## [00:00 - 03:02]

This is the second part of the audience spotlighting strategy and what I'm going to take you through is once you've done your audience research, you've got your interests, you've got your targeting groups, is actually how you then take those forward and test them. One of the key parts of this is using page post engagement for testing. So there's different preferences and strategies out there from different people that do Facebook advertising on how to actually test audiences. What I prefer to do is to use page post engagement for my testing because you have a far wider reach within any given audience of finding potential people who are going to match your audience. And what I mean by that is let's say you have a pool of a million people in your audience, the people who are more likely to be purchasers, maybe as part of your website conversion campaign, it's going to be far smaller than those that are likely to engage. And it's those engages that give us good signals faster and allows us to test with smaller spends a lot quicker to actually be able to determine whether an interest group is actually worth our while or not. So what I do is to split the audience into separate assets, first of all. Now the audience could be an interest. It could be a behavior, could be an interest behavior pair, could be a lookalike or a customer audience, especially with interests. When you split them into separate ad sets, it's kind of better to get the detail on what's working and what's not with the click through rate CPC by interest. So if you were, for example, to group 10 interests into one ad set, how do you know which one of those or which ones of those interests are actually working for you? Maybe a few that get an interest, impressions that are not quite working for you, they're not quite your target market. There are tools that allow you to use the Facebook ads API to pull out detail within a single ad set and look at multiple interests within that to see what's happening. But the easiest way, especially as you're testing iteratively and quite quickly, is to pull them into different ad sets and test those out as well. And also when I'm doing audience testing, I'm testing multiple different creatives within that as well. So if I have, let's say, 10 interests that I've pulled out into 10 ad sets, the worst thing would be to run the same ad in every single ad set because the problem there is you don't know whether it's the ad or the interest that's not working. I'll give you an example. We were doing some ads for a health brand and they were selling food, nutritional food, etc. And we were testing ads that were targeting people based on food interests, nutrition interest, etc. And then also targeting people based on fashion. So what we found through dissecting their audiences that were that people did have

## [03:02 - 06:06]

interest in other food brands, but they also had wider interests in fashion and brands and things like that as well. So we split those out into different interest groups and we ran multiple ads through that as well. And what we found was some of the ads that were working on, let's say the food related brands or the interests didn't quite work as well as different ads which were targeting fashion conscious people. And we changed up the copy, slightly changed up the creative as well for these. And it was a good way of actually properly testing whether an ad ad set with an interest was actually working or not. And what we do is once we find an interest that's working, graduate that from page post engagement up into your website conversion campaign. So it really depends on what your strategy is and how you're looking to push and scale your spend up, whether it's website conversion, could be video views, could be a lead gen campaign, but this is a great way of spending, you know, from $5 a day, $10 a day, $50 a day per interest and in your testing to actually find opportunities to scale up. So I'll give you a few examples of how we've used this. So the example up here is that company that I mentioned where we were targeting some food brands and also some fashion and kind of clothing brands as well. And actually what I wanted to highlight here is when we were running a set of three ads, three very similar ads to all of these interest groups, we found that the ads just didn't perform for these three interests here. So you might look at this and say, you know what, these interests, they're not worth our time. The click through rate and CPC were not great. And you look at these two guys here, the click through rate was good. So we have good indicators that these are kind of more closer to what we're looking for. But actually what we then did is to test different creatives here and those, those different creatives actually perform better as well. So sometimes with the interests, you have to kind of give different angles and different approaches to how you're going to communicate to them to try and see if the interest group, the targeting is actually interesting in what you're offering or not before you give up on it and before you kind of move on. One of the key things about this kind of testing approach is when we're testing creatives, we're testing a range of static carousel, potentially video to see whether the format plays a part as well. And you know, it's not always about the interest working or not working. You have to get the interest and the creative working together and hand in hand to actually make this test kind of scientific, I guess. Also, when, when I'm running a page post engagement campaign, whilst a lot of people will look at the cost per engagement, I'm actually looking at the click through rate and also the cost per click reason being, and I've seen this numerous times is you may get ads and audiences, which give you a really, really low cost per engagement of 0.1 cents.

## [06:07 - 09:07]

But when you look at how many people are actually clicking and what their actions are off of that ad, they're not really doing much else. And so what you end up doing is attracting people who are likely to like and share and, you know, it's a nice ad. They're pleasantly interested in what's going on, but they're not actually clicking to find out more. One of the misconceptions about running a page post engagement campaign is that you only get people that would like, share and comment. And this is not the case. Time and time again, when I'm testing and I, I'm running interests and running creatives, I'll run ads on page post engagements that are getting clicks, are getting ads to carts, and are getting purchases as well. And so it would be a kind of misnomer to actually overlook page post engagement for testing something for website conversion, or lead gen, et cetera. So that's why I look at click through rate and cost per click with my page post engagement testing because of the type of people in there are also potentially purchases. And they're also potentially be there in market. It doesn't mean that just because it's a page post engagement objective and a campaign that you won't find a purchase. I guarantee you there are purchases in that audience. And a lot of it depends on how well you're targeting and you're creative is placed out as well. The second example here that I want to give is actually for a digital course, a digital product that we're selling. So it's not just about your commerce. And we pulled out four audiences that we wanted to test. And using the same page post engagement approach, this one was pure video. And we're trying to push the video to create a new video views custom audience. And what we are measuring here was actually the cost per 10 second video view engagement. So a lot of you will look at the kind of video engagement metrics and maybe not be quite sure of which one to pin your metrics close to. Facebook include, for example, the three second video view metric. For me, a lot of people will accidentally view or maybe after three seconds realize it's not for them. If they get to 10 seconds, for me, that's a strong indicator that they are actually interested in the video. And so the cost for getting them to that 10 second view is a good indicator. And also for video, I'm looking at the click through all and the cost per click all metric as well. The difference between the all and link link click is click through rate all that looks at the clicks across the whole piece. So for example, in this specific test, we had copy which expanded below the read more link. So if someone clicks on the read ball, that counts as a click. So I want to know that people are actually clicking the read more. That for me is a valuable action. So again, I'm kind of looking at this example here. I'm looking at the click through rate all cost per click all and looking at what that 10 second engagement rate looks like as well. And, you know, for this example here, we have one audience which generally failed across all of those metrics. It might not look like a lot in terms of the difference, but this is enough to tell me that this audience didn't work.

## [09:07 - 09:55]

We may go back and test different creators, but we have a couple of audiences here that actually did work a lot better for this specific product that we were selling. And so the real key thing here is once you've done your audience research, test in individual ad sets, put your kind of single interests in against your key demographic, test multiple creatives, at least three creatives, ideally three very different creatives and keep an eye on your key metrics depending on the type of creative. So click through at CPC. If it's more kind of a direct response type of ad and maybe you've got a link in the body as well. And, you know, for the video views, making sure that people are getting to 10 seconds because that's a valuable action as well.
